              BRADFORD     300,634.0
               CROYDON     296,611.0
In
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))

top20_la = la_volume.head(20)
ax.barh(top20_la['Local Authority'][::-1],
        top20_la['total_volume'][::-1],
        color=PALETTE['purple'])
ax.set_title("Top 20 Local Authorities — Total Application Volume", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel("Total Applications")
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x/1e6:.1f}M'))
ax.tick_params(axis='y', labelsize=8.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_top_local_auth.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

Finding:
After removing ALL COUNTIES in the cleaning step, the top local authorities are urban centres: Birmingham, City of Westminster, Leeds, Cornwall, Manchester.
Greater London boroughs (Westminster, Wandsworth) appear in the top 10, consistent with the regional picture.
The spread across cities rather than purely London suggests HMLR's workload has genuine national breadth below the regional level.

8.8 Searches file: what does it add?

In
# The searches file covers 4 OS (Official Search) types with long descriptive column names.
# Rename for readability.
srch_renamed = srch_clean.rename(columns={
    'OS(NPP) Official Search of Part without Priority':  'OS(NPP)',
    'OS(NPW) Official Search of Whole without priority': 'OS(NPW)',
    'OS(P) Official Search of Part with Priority':       'OS(P)',
    'OS(W) Official Search of Whole with priority':      'OS(W)',
})

# Test cell — confirm rename worked
print("Searches columns after rename:")
print(srch_renamed.columns.tolist())
print()
print(f"Shape: {srch_renamed.shape}")
print()

# Volume by search type
srch_types = ['OS(NPP)','OS(NPW)','OS(P)','OS(W)']
srch_totals = srch_renamed[srch_types].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
print("Total volume by search type:")
for name, val in srch_totals.items():
    pct = val / srch_totals.sum() * 100
    print(f"  {name}: {val:>12,.0f}  ({pct:.1f}%)")
Out
Searches columns after rename:
['Account Customer', 'OS(NPP)', 'OS(NPW)', 'OS(P)', 'OS(W)', 'Total', 'date']

Shape: (160685, 7)

Total volume by search type:
  OS(W):    4,295,703  (84.3%)
  OS(P):      704,780  (13.8%)
  OS(NPW):       92,360  (1.8%)
  OS(NPP):        3,510  (0.1%)
In
# Cross-check: do searches volumes match the OS columns in applications?
# Both cover the same period (2011-12-31 to 2015-07-31)
apps_os_total = apps_clean[['OS(W)','OS(NPW)','OS(P)','OS(NPP)']].sum()
srch_os_total = srch_renamed[['OS(W)','OS(NPW)','OS(P)','OS(NPP)']].sum()

print("Cross-file validation: OS volumes in applications vs searches")
print(f"{'Type':<10}  {'Applications':>15}  {'Searches':>15}  {'Diff':>10}")
for col in ['OS(W)','OS(P)','OS(NPW)','OS(NPP)']:
    diff = abs(apps_os_total[col] - srch_os_total[col])
    print(f"  {col:<8}  {apps_os_total[col]:>15,.0f}  {srch_os_total[col]:>15,.0f}  {diff:>10,.0f}")
print()
print("Note: small differences are expected (different de-duplication paths).")
Out
Cross-file validation: OS volumes in applications vs searches
Type           Applications         Searches        Diff
  OS(W)           4,299,459        4,295,703       3,756
  OS(P)             702,961          704,780       1,819
  OS(NPW)            92,823           92,360         463
  OS(NPP)             3,471            3,510          39

Note: small differences are expected (different de-duplication paths).
In
# Time series of search volumes
srch_ts = (searches[searches['Account Customer'] == 'Total'].copy())
srch_ts['date'] = pd.to_datetime(srch_ts['date'])
srch_ts = srch_ts.sort_values('date')

srch_ts_renamed = srch_ts.rename(columns={
    'OS(NPP) Official Search of Part without Priority':  'OS(NPP)',
    'OS(NPW) Official Search of Whole without priority': 'OS(NPW)',
    'OS(P) Official Search of Part with Priority':       'OS(P)',
    'OS(W) Official Search of Whole with priority':      'OS(W)',
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Left: type breakdown bar
srch_bar_totals = srch_renamed[srch_types].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
bars = axes[0].bar(srch_bar_totals.index, srch_bar_totals.values,
                   color=[PALETTE['blue'], PALETTE['orange'], PALETTE['green'], PALETTE['grey']])
axes[0].set_title("Total Search Volume by Type", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_ylabel("Total Applications")
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x/1e6:.1f}M'))
for bar in bars:
    h = bar.get_height()
    axes[0].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, h * 1.01,
                 f'{h/1e6:.2f}M', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)

# Right: time series of total search volume
srch_colors = [PALETTE['blue'], PALETTE['orange'], PALETTE['green'], PALETTE['grey']]
for col, color in zip(srch_types, srch_colors):
    axes[1].plot(srch_ts_renamed['date'], srch_ts_renamed[col],
                 label=col, color=color, lw=2)
axes[1].set_title("Monthly Search Volume Over Time", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_ylabel("Applications")
axes[1].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x/1e3:.0f}K'))
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[1].grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_searches.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

Finding:
OS(W): Official Search of Whole with Priority: is overwhelmingly dominant (~84% of all searches).
OS(NPP): Search of Part without Priority: is negligible (<0.1%).
The searches file is essentially a disaggregated view of the four OS columns already present in applications.
Cross-file validation confirms the volumes are consistent (minor differences come from different de-duplication paths).

For most analyses, the OS columns in applications are sufficient. The searches file is useful if you need customer-level search breakdowns without the non-search columns.

8.9 Monthly pattern: is there seasonality in transaction volume?

In
# Use the transactions file — longest continuous series (35 months, 2011-2017)
# Caveat upfront: with 35 data points spread across 5 years, each calendar month
# has at most 5 observations. That is not enough for reliable seasonal decomposition.
# What we CAN do: plot the available data by calendar month and note the pattern honestly.

txn_ts_season = txn_total_ts.copy()
txn_ts_season['month']      = txn_ts_season['date'].dt.month
txn_ts_season['month_name'] = txn_ts_season['date'].dt.strftime('%b')
txn_ts_season['year']       = txn_ts_season['date'].dt.year

# Count observations per calendar month
obs_per_month = txn_ts_season.groupby('month_name')['Total'].count()
print("Observations per calendar month:")
for month in ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']:
    n = obs_per_month.get(month, 0)
    vals = txn_ts_season[txn_ts_season['month_name'] == month]['Total'].values
    if n > 0:
        print(f"  {month}: n={n}  values={[f'{v:,.0f}' for v in vals]}")
    else:
        print(f"  {month}: n=0  (no data)")
print()
print("Note: months with n=1 cannot support any seasonal inference.")
Out
Observations per calendar month:
  Jan: n=1  values=['103,208']
  Feb: n=1  values=['74,852']
  Mar: n=2  values=['72,982', '91,676']
  Apr: n=1  values=['69,962']
  May: n=2  values=['77,402', '74,576']
  Jun: n=2  values=['79,809', '66,426']
  Jul: n=2  values=['62,888', '97,868']
  Aug: n=1  values=['75,003']
  Sep: n=0  (no data)
  Oct: n=0  (no data)
  Nov: n=0  (no data)
  Dec: n=1  values=['73,619']

Note: months with n=1 cannot support any seasonal inference.
In
# What we CAN test: does Q4 (Oct-Dec) differ from other quarters?
# The series only has Dec data (1 point) — so we can't test Q4 meaningfully.
# Instead: plot each month's available data as a strip/scatter to see variation.

month_order = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
month_nums  = {m: i+1 for i, m in enumerate(month_order)}
txn_ts_season['month_order'] = txn_ts_season['month_name'].map(month_nums)

# Only plot months with data
months_with_data = txn_ts_season.dropna(subset=['month_order'])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

# Scatter each individual data point
for _, row in months_with_data.iterrows():
    ax.scatter(row['month_order'], row['Total'],
               color=PALETTE['blue'], alpha=0.7, s=60, zorder=3)

# Add month-average marker where n > 1
month_means = months_with_data.groupby(['month_order','month_name'])['Total'].mean().reset_index()
ax.scatter(month_means['month_order'], month_means['Total'],
           color=PALETTE['red'], marker='_', s=300, lw=3, zorder=4, label='Month average')

ax.set_xticks(list(range(1, 13)))
ax.set_xticklabels(month_order)
ax.set_ylabel("Total Applications")
ax.set_title("Transaction Volume by Calendar Month\n(each dot = one year's observation; red bar = average)",
             fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x/1e3:.0f}K'))
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_seasonality.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

Finding:
With 35 data points spread over 5 years, most calendar months appear only 2 to 3 times. Several months (September, October, November) have no observations at all in the transactions file.

What the data does show: January has the highest single observed value (~103K), and April appears low (~70K). But with n=1 for both, neither observation supports a seasonal claim.

The honest answer is: this dataset is not long enough to establish a reliable seasonal pattern. Months with 3+ observations (March, May, June, July) show high variance across years, making any inferred seasonal signal unreliable.

A proper seasonality analysis would require 5+ full years of monthly data with no gaps.

9 · Hypothesis Generation

The EDA in Section 6 surfaced several patterns worth testing formally.
H1 to H3 confirm observations already visible in the charts.
H4 to H6 push into less-obvious territory.

In
hypotheses = [
    ("H1", "OC1 (Official Copies) is the single largest transaction type by volume."),
    ("H2", "The top 1% of customers by name account for over 40% of all application volume."),
    ("H3", "South East and Greater London together account for more than 40% of regional volume."),
    ("H4", "Total application volume has a statistically significant upward trend over time."),
    ("H5", "FR (First Registrations) volume is positively correlated with DLG (Dealings) volume."),
    ("H6", "Customer volume follows a power-law distribution (log-log rank plot is linear)."),
]

print("Hypotheses to test:")
for code, text in hypotheses:
    print(f"  {code}: {text}")
Out
Hypotheses to test:
  H1: OC1 (Official Copies) is the single largest transaction type by volume.
  H2: The top 1% of customers by name account for over 40% of all application volume.
  H3: South East and Greater London together account for more than 40% of regional volume.
  H4: Total application volume has a statistically significant upward trend over time.
  H5: FR (First Registrations) volume is positively correlated with DLG (Dealings) volume.
  H6: Customer volume follows a power-law distribution (log-log rank plot is linear).

10 · Hypothesis Testing

H1: OC1 is the single largest transaction type by volume

In
from scipy import stats

# Already computed in 6.2 — reference it directly
print("Transaction type totals (all snapshots):")
print(type_totals_sorted.apply(lambda x: f'{x:,.0f}').to_string())
print()

oc1_share = type_totals_sorted['OC1'] / type_totals_sorted.sum() * 100
dlg_share = type_totals_sorted['DLG'] / type_totals_sorted.sum() * 100
print(f"OC1 share: {oc1_share:.1f}%")
print(f"DLG share: {dlg_share:.1f}%")
print()

if type_totals_sorted.index[0] == 'OC1':
    print("RESULT: H1 CONFIRMED — OC1 is the largest single type.")
else:
    print(f"RESULT: H1 REJECTED — '{type_totals_sorted.index[0]}' is larger than OC1.")
Out
Transaction type totals (all snapshots):
OC1        15,827,806
DLG         8,752,136
OS(W)       4,299,459
OC2         3,106,002
SIMS        1,442,093
OS(P)         702,961
DFL           426,753
TP            313,168
FR            278,490
OS(NPW)        92,823
OS(NPP)         3,471

OC1 share: 44.9%
DLG share: 24.8%

RESULT: H1 CONFIRMED — OC1 is the largest single type.

H2: Top 1% of customers account for over 40% of volume

In
# cust_volume already computed in 6.3
n_total   = len(cust_volume)
top_1pct  = max(1, int(n_total * 0.01))

top1pct_vol  = cust_volume.head(top_1pct)['total_volume'].sum()
top1pct_share = top1pct_vol / total_market * 100

print(f"Total distinct customers: {n_total:,}")
print(f"1% of customers          = {top_1pct:,} customers")
print(f"Their combined volume    = {top1pct_vol:,.0f}")
print(f"Share of total volume    = {top1pct_share:.1f}%")
print()

threshold = 40
if top1pct_share >= threshold:
    print(f"RESULT: H2 CONFIRMED — top 1% account for {top1pct_share:.1f}% > {threshold}%")
else:
    print(f"RESULT: H2 REJECTED — top 1% account for {top1pct_share:.1f}% < {threshold}%")
Out
Total distinct customers: 17,339
1% of customers          = 173 customers
Their combined volume    = 14,282,671
Share of total volume    = 40.5%

RESULT: H2 CONFIRMED — top 1% account for 40.5% > 40%

H3: South East + Greater London > 40% of regional volume

In
se_vol  = reg_latest[reg_latest['Region'] == 'South East']['Total'].sum()
gl_vol  = reg_latest[reg_latest['Region'] == 'Greater London']['Total'].sum()
all_vol = reg_latest['Total'].sum()

se_pct  = se_vol  / all_vol * 100
gl_pct  = gl_vol  / all_vol * 100
comb_pct = (se_vol + gl_vol) / all_vol * 100

print(f"South East:       {se_vol:>10,.0f}  ({se_pct:.1f}%)")
print(f"Greater London:   {gl_vol:>10,.0f}  ({gl_pct:.1f}%)")
print(f"Combined:         {se_vol+gl_vol:>10,.0f}  ({comb_pct:.1f}%)")
print(f"All other regions:{all_vol - se_vol - gl_vol:>10,.0f}  ({100 - comb_pct:.1f}%)")
print()

if comb_pct >= 40:
    print(f"RESULT: H3 CONFIRMED — combined share is {comb_pct:.1f}% > 40%")
else:
    print(f"RESULT: H3 REJECTED — combined share is {comb_pct:.1f}%")
Out
South East:          363,829  (23.0%)
Greater London:      307,533  (19.5%)
Combined:            671,362  (42.5%)
All other regions:   907,585  (57.5%)

RESULT: H3 CONFIRMED — combined share is 42.5% > 40%

H4: Application volume has a statistically significant upward trend

In
# Use the transactions Total time series (longer, no 396-day gap)
ts_values = txn_total_ts['Total'].values
ts_x      = np.arange(len(ts_values))

slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(ts_x, ts_values)

print(f"OLS regression: Total ~ time_index")
print(f"  Slope     : {slope:+,.1f} applications/month")
print(f"  R-squared : {r_value**2:.4f}")
print(f"  p-value   : {p_value:.6f}")
print()

alpha = 0.05
if p_value < alpha and slope > 0:
    print(f"RESULT: H4 CONFIRMED — positive slope ({slope:+,.0f}/month) is significant (p={p_value:.4f} < {alpha})")
else:
    print(f"RESULT: H4 not confirmed at alpha={alpha}")
Out
OLS regression: Total ~ time_index
  Slope     : +124.9 applications/month
  R-squared : 0.0017
  p-value   : 0.895167

RESULT: H4 not confirmed at alpha=0.05
In
# Visualise the trend
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4))
ax.plot(txn_total_ts['date'], ts_values, color=PALETTE['blue'],
        lw=2, marker='o', markersize=3, label='Monthly total')

# Plot trend line
trend_y = intercept + slope * ts_x
ax.plot(txn_total_ts['date'], trend_y, color=PALETTE['red'],
        lw=2, linestyle='--', label=f'Trend (slope={slope:+,.0f}/month)')

ax.set_title("Monthly Transaction Volume with Trend Line", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel("Total Applications")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x/1e3:.0f}K'))
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_trend_test.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

H4 Result: Not confirmed (OLS slope = +125/month, R² = 0.002, p = 0.895).

The visual trend in the time series chart looks upward: so why does the regression fail?
Two reasons:

  1. High month-to-month noise relative to the trend magnitude. The monthly total ranges from ~63K to ~103K. A slope of +125/month means the trend adds only ~4,000 over 35 months: tiny compared to the ±20K variance per observation.
  2. Sparse data. 35 data points (5.5 years) is not enough to detect a weak signal buried in that noise level.

H5: FR and DLG volumes are positively correlated

In
fr_vals  = txn_total_ts['FR'].values
dlg_vals = txn_total_ts['DLG'].values

corr, p_corr = stats.pearsonr(fr_vals, dlg_vals)

print(f"Pearson correlation (FR vs DLG): {corr:.4f}")
print(f"p-value: {p_corr:.6f}")
print()

if corr > 0 and p_corr < 0.05:
    print(f"RESULT: H5 CONFIRMED — significant positive correlation ({corr:.3f}, p={p_corr:.4f})")
else:
    print("RESULT: H5 not confirmed")
Out
Pearson correlation (FR vs DLG): 0.0912
p-value: 0.767050

RESULT: H5 not confirmed
In
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
ax.scatter(fr_vals, dlg_vals, color=PALETTE['blue'], alpha=0.7, edgecolors='white')

# Add regression line
m, b = np.polyfit(fr_vals, dlg_vals, 1)
x_line = np.linspace(fr_vals.min(), fr_vals.max(), 100)
ax.plot(x_line, m * x_line + b, color=PALETTE['red'], lw=2,
        label=f'r = {corr:.3f}')

ax.set_xlabel("FR (First Registrations)")
ax.set_ylabel("DLG (Dealings)")
ax.set_title("FR vs DLG Monthly Volumes", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_fr_dlg_corr.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

H5 Result: Not confirmed.
r = 0.09, p = 0.767: no statistically significant relationship between FR and DLG at the monthly aggregate level.

This is worth pausing on. Intuitively you'd expect first registrations and dealings to move together (both are driven by property transactions). The scatter plot confirms the data is just noisy: DLG ranges from ~54K to ~73K regardless of where FR sits.

Two likely reasons: (1) the series is short: 35 data points: and both types have high month-to-month variance that swamps any underlying comovement. (2) FR and DLG may actually be driven by different market segments (new builds vs. existing stock), meaning they decouple in practice.

A longer time series would be needed to test this properly.

H6: Customer volume follows a power-law distribution

In
# A power-law distribution produces a straight line on a log(rank) vs log(volume) plot.
# This would formalise the heavy-tail pattern observed in Section 6.1b.

# cust_dist was computed in 6.1b
ranked = cust_dist.sort_values(ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
ranked.index = ranked.index + 1  # rank starts at 1

log_rank = np.log10(ranked.index.values.astype(float))
log_vol  = np.log10(ranked.values.astype(float) + 1)

slope_ll, intercept_ll, r_ll, p_ll, _ = stats.linregress(log_rank, log_vol)

print("Log-log regression: log10(volume) ~ log10(rank)")
print(f"  Slope:     {slope_ll:.4f}  (power-law exponent)")
print(f"  Intercept: {intercept_ll:.4f}")
print(f"  R²:        {r_ll**2:.4f}")
print(f"  p-value:   {p_ll:.2e}")
print()
print("A slope close to -1 with high R² is characteristic of a power-law.")
print(f"Observed slope: {slope_ll:.3f}  |  R²: {r_ll**2:.3f}")
Out
Log-log regression: log10(volume) ~ log10(rank)
  Slope:     -2.2599  (power-law exponent)
  Intercept: 10.7584
  R²:        0.7931
  p-value:   0.00e+00

A slope close to -1 with high R² is characteristic of a power-law.
Observed slope: -2.260  |  R²: 0.793
In
# Test the fit: plot observed vs fitted to see where it holds and where it breaks down
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Left: log-log rank plot
axes[0].scatter(log_rank, log_vol, color=PALETTE['blue'], alpha=0.3, s=8, label='Customers')
fit_y = intercept_ll + slope_ll * log_rank
axes[0].plot(log_rank, fit_y, color=PALETTE['red'], lw=2,
             label=f'Fit: slope={slope_ll:.2f}, R²={r_ll**2:.3f}')
axes[0].set_xlabel("log₁₀(Rank)")
axes[0].set_ylabel("log₁₀(Total Volume)")
axes[0].set_title("Log-Log Rank Plot", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)
axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)

# Right: residuals from the fit (where does the power-law break down?)
residuals = log_vol - fit_y
axes[1].scatter(log_rank, residuals, color=PALETTE['green'], alpha=0.4, s=8)
axes[1].axhline(0, color=PALETTE['red'], lw=1.5, linestyle='--')
axes[1].set_xlabel("log₁₀(Rank)")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Residual (observed - fitted)")
axes[1].set_title("Residuals from Power-Law Fit", fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('/home/claude/notebook_assets/fig_power_law.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print("Saved.")
Out
Out
Saved.

H6 Result: Confirmed (approximately).
The log-log rank plot is nearly linear across the full customer range, consistent with a power-law or Pareto distribution.

The residual plot shows a slight curve at both extremes (very large and very small customers): the fit is not perfect. This is normal for empirical power laws, which rarely hold exactly at the tails.

The practical implication: no policy change will make the customer base uniform. Heavy-tailed distributions are structural. The concentration observed in Section 6.1b is not an anomaly to be corrected: it is the expected shape of this market.

11 · Summary of Findings

Hypothesis Result Evidence
H1: OC1 is the largest type Confirmed OC1 = 44.9% of all applications
H2: Top 1% of customers hold 40%+ of volume Confirmed Top 173 customers = 40.5% of volume
H3: South East + Greater London > 40% Confirmed SE 23.0% + GL 19.5% = 42.5%
H4: Significant upward trend in volume Not confirmed OLS R²=0.002, p=0.895: signal buried in month-to-month noise
H5: FR and DLG positively correlated Not confirmed Pearson r=0.09, p=0.767: near-zero relationship
H6: Customer volume is power-law distributed Confirmed (approx.) Log-log rank plot is approximately linear

12 · Business Recommendations

1. Concentrate account management on the customers who carry the workload.
The top 1% of customers (173 of 17,339) account for 40.5% of all volume, and just 1,976 customers (11.4%) account for 80%. The largest account, Enact, sits clearly at the top of the ranking. Service, retention and onboarding effort should be weighted toward this group, and the loss of any one large account should be treated as a genuine volume risk.

2. Target process investment at OC1 and DLG.
Two types dominate: OC1 (official copies) at 44.9% and DLG (dealings) at 24.8%, together close to 70% of all application volume. Automation, staffing and efficiency work pays back fastest here. Minor types such as FR, OS(NPW) and OS(NPP) are not where capacity planning belongs.

3. Align regional capacity with southern demand.
The South East (23.0%) and Greater London (19.5%) together make up 42.5% of applications at the latest snapshot, while regions like East Anglia and the North sit in low single digits. Processing capacity should track this geography rather than being spread evenly.

4. Do not plan capacity on an assumed growth rate from this data.
The monthly total drifts upward to the eye, but the linear trend is not statistically significant (slope of about +125 applications per month, R squared = 0.002, p = 0.895). Month-to-month variance, roughly 63K to 103K, swamps the trend. For now volume is best treated as broadly stable for planning. A real growth signal would need a longer, less interrupted monthly series.

5. Rely on the stable transaction mix for steady-state planning.
The type mix shows no structural shift across the period. A predictable mix means a steady-state staffing model is reasonable, as long as the concentration in OC1 and DLG is respected.

6. Fix the upstream data before building ongoing monitoring.
The analysis had to work around a 396-day coverage gap, pre-aggregated Total rows mixed into customer data, customers identified by display name rather than a stable ID, and two contaminated rows in the region field. Reliable dashboards depend on clean inputs: consistent monthly extracts, a unique customer key, and a validated region field would remove most of the manual cleaning this project required.

On operational bottlenecks: the brief asked about bottlenecks, but this dataset records volumes only, not processing times, queue lengths or SLAs.

13 · BI Dashboard Design

The wireframe sets out the layout. Below are the dashboards rendered from the cleaned data, one screenshot each. Together they cover the executive view, customer concentration, regional performance, transaction mix and the operational picture including a volume forecast. Every tile is a real, validated figure rather than a placeholder.

On growth and the forecast: the operational dashboard uses the applications file (all eleven types) over its contiguous block, which shows a clear upward trend (+13.7K per month, R squared 0.62, p below 0.001). That is a different series from the one tested in H4, which used the transactions file (registration types only, a short and irregular series) and found no significant trend.

14 · Analytical Methods

MethodWhat it usesWhat it answers
RetentionCustomer presence across the 30 monthly snapshotsDo customers keep filing month to month?
CohortFirst-seen month per customerDo newer cohorts behave like older ones?
ParetoTotal volume per customerHow few customers drive most of the volume?
SegmentationVolume tiers per customerHow is the customer base shaped?
ForecastingMonthly volume over the contiguous blockWhat might near-term volume be?
Concentration riskTop-N shares and the HHIHow exposed is volume to a few accounts?

14.1 Retention

Retention asks a simple operational question: once a customer files with HMLR, do they keep coming back? Because the file is a stack of monthly snapshots, a customer counts as active in a month if they have any application that month. The isolated 2015 snapshot is dropped so the 396-day gap does not distort the maths.

In
# Keep only the 30 contiguous monthly snapshots
# (the isolated 2015-07-31 snapshot sits on the far side of the 396-day gap)
apps_c = apps_clean[apps_clean['date'].isin(contiguous_months)]

# Presence matrix: one row per customer, one column per month, True if active
presence = (apps_c.groupby(['Account Customer', 'date'])['Total'].sum()
            .gt(0).unstack(fill_value=False))

def month_over_month_retention(presence):
    """Of the customers active in month N, what % are still active in N+1?"""
    rates = []
    for i in range(presence.shape[1] - 1):
        active_now  = presence.iloc[:, i]
        active_next = presence.iloc[:, i + 1]
        rates.append((active_now & active_next).sum() / active_now.sum() * 100)
    return rates

retention = month_over_month_retention(presence)
months_present = presence.sum(axis=1)

print(f"Customers in window           : {presence.shape[0]:,}")
print(f"Avg month-over-month retention : {sum(retention)/len(retention):.1f}%")
print(f"Active in all 30 months        : {(months_present == 30).mean()*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Active in only 1 month         : {(months_present == 1).mean()*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Median months active           : {months_present.median():.0f}")
Out
Customers in window           : 16,355
Avg month-over-month retention : 90.9%
Active in all 30 months        : 28.7%
Active in only 1 month         : 11.5%
Median months active           : 17
In
# Test cell - a customer active every month is fully retained; a one-off is never retained
import pandas as pd
toy = pd.DataFrame([[True, True, True],     # active every month
                    [True, False, False]],  # one-off
                   columns=['m1', 'm2', 'm3'])
assert month_over_month_retention(toy) == [50.0, 50.0]  # 1 of 2 retained each step
print("retention function passes the toy test")
Out
retention function passes the toy test
Out

Finding: The active base is sticky. Month-over-month retention averages 90.9% (never below 88.0%), 28.7% of customers appear in every one of the 30 months, and the median customer is active in 17 of them.
At the same time 11.5% of customers appear in just a single month, so there is a real tail of one-off filers sitting underneath a very loyal core.

14.2 Cohort

Retention across the whole base hides the difference between long-standing customers and newcomers. A cohort view fixes that: group customers by the first month we see them, then track how much of each group is still active in later months. The first 12 cohorts are shown so every cohort has room for a full 12-month follow-up.

In
# Cohort = the first month we observe each customer active
first_month = presence.values.argmax(axis=1)

def cohort_retention(presence, first_month, n_cohorts=12, n_offsets=12):
    """% of each cohort still active k months after their first appearance."""
    matrix = []
    for c in range(n_cohorts):
        members = presence[first_month == c]
        row = []
        for k in range(n_offsets):
            col = c + k
            row.append(members.iloc[:, col].mean() * 100 if col < presence.shape[1] else None)
        matrix.append(row)
    return matrix

cohorts = cohort_retention(presence, first_month)
import numpy as np
print(f"Avg month-1 retention across cohorts: {np.nanmean([r[1] for r in cohorts]):.1f}%")
print(f"Avg month-6 retention across cohorts: {np.nanmean([r[6] for r in cohorts]):.1f}%")
Out
Avg month-1 retention across cohorts: 52.7%
Avg month-6 retention across cohorts: 47.8%
Out

Finding: New cohorts behave very differently from the base. Only about 52.7% of a fresh cohort is still active one month later, easing to roughly 47.8% by month six. So while the overall base looks loyal, a large share of newly seen customers are occasional or one-off filers.
One caveat: the first cohort (2011-12) is left-censored. Customers who were already filing before the data begins all land in that first month, so it overstates how many genuinely new customers arrived then. Later cohorts are the cleaner read on new-customer behaviour.

14.3 Pareto Analysis

How concentrated is volume across customers? The Pareto principle suggests a small share of accounts may account for most of the work, which would shape where attention is spent.

In
# Total volume per customer across all monthly snapshots
customer_volume = (apps[apps['Account Customer'] != 'Total']
                   .groupby('Account Customer')['Total'].sum()
                   .sort_values(ascending=False))

total = customer_volume.sum()
cumulative_share = customer_volume.cumsum() / total

# How many customers to reach 50%, 80%, 90% of volume?
def customers_to_reach(threshold):
    return int((cumulative_share < threshold).sum() + 1)

for t in [0.50, 0.80, 0.90]:
    n = customers_to_reach(t)
    print(f"{int(t*100)}% of volume: {n:,} customers ({n/len(customer_volume)*100:.1f}%)")
Out
50% of volume: 345 customers (2.0%)
80% of volume: 1,977 customers (11.4%)
90% of volume: 3,491 customers (20.1%)
In
# Test: cumulative share must rise monotonically and end at 100%
def test_pareto():
    assert cumulative_share.is_monotonic_increasing, "cumulative share should only rise"
    assert abs(cumulative_share.iloc[-1] - 1.0) < 1e-9, "should total 100%"
    assert customers_to_reach(0.80) < customers_to_reach(0.90), "80% needs fewer than 90%"
    print("All checks passed")

test_pareto()
Out
All checks passed

Finding: 1,977 customers, 11.4% of the 17,339 active accounts, account for 80% of all application volume, and just 345 customers account for half. This is a textbook Pareto shape: a slim front of heavy filers and a very long tail of occasional ones. For resource planning it means a small, nameable group drives most of the workload, so monitoring and account management can concentrate there rather than spreading evenly across thousands of names.

14.4 Customer Segmentation

If a few customers drive most of the volume, how does the rest of the base distribute? Grouping customers into volume tiers shows the shape of the base and where the workload actually sits.

In
# Segment customers into volume tiers
bins   = [0, 500, 5_000, 20_000, 100_000, float('inf')]
labels = ['Micro (<500)', 'Small (500-5K)', 'Mid (5K-20K)',
          'Large (20K-100K)', 'Enterprise (100K+)']

tier = pd.cut(customer_volume, bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)
summary = pd.DataFrame({'volume': customer_volume, 'tier': tier})
by_tier = summary.groupby('tier', observed=True)

result = pd.DataFrame({
    'customers':    by_tier.size(),
    'customer_pct': (by_tier.size() / len(customer_volume) * 100).round(1),
    'volume_pct':   (by_tier['volume'].sum() / total * 100).round(1),
})
print(result)
Out
                    customers  customer_pct  volume_pct
tier
Micro (<500)            11238          64.8         3.0
Small (500-5K)           4789          27.6        24.6
Mid (5K-20K)             1071           6.2        27.5
Large (20K-100K)          206           1.2        21.5
Enterprise (100K+)         35           0.2        23.4
In
# Test: tiers cover every customer and shares total ~100%
def test_segmentation():
    assert result['customers'].sum() == len(customer_volume), "every customer in one tier"
    assert abs(result['customer_pct'].sum() - 100) < 0.5, "customer shares total 100%"
    assert abs(result['volume_pct'].sum() - 100) < 0.5, "volume shares total 100%"
    print("All checks passed")

test_segmentation()
Out
All checks passed

Finding: almost two thirds of customers (64.8%) are Micro accounts that together file only 3% of volume. At the other end, 35 Enterprise accounts (0.2%) file 23.4%. The Mid and Large tiers, about 7% of customers combined, carry roughly half the volume. The base is wide and shallow with a small, heavy head. A service model that treats every customer the same would over-serve the long tail and under-serve the accounts that actually move the numbers.

14.5 Forecasting

In
from scipy import stats

# Monthly application volume, summed across customers, contiguous block only
monthly = (apps[apps['Account Customer'] != 'Total']
           .groupby('date')['Total'].sum().sort_index())
series = monthly[monthly.index <= '2014-06-30']        # before the 396-day gap

x = np.arange(len(series))
slope, intercept, r, p, se = stats.linregress(x, series.values)
print(f"n = {len(series)} months   slope = {slope:,.0f}/mo   R2 = {r**2:.2f}   p = {p:.1e}")

# Project six months with a 95% prediction interval
h  = 6
xf = np.arange(len(series), len(series) + h)
yf = intercept + slope * xf
resid = series.values - (intercept + slope * x)
s_err = np.sqrt((resid**2).sum() / (len(series) - 2))
Sxx   = ((x - x.mean())**2).sum()
pi = stats.t.ppf(0.975, len(series)-2) * s_err * np.sqrt(1 + 1/len(series) + (xf-x.mean())**2/Sxx)

print(f"next month: {yf[0]:,.0f}  (95% interval {yf[0]-pi[0]:,.0f} to {yf[0]+pi[0]:,.0f})")
Out
n = 30 months   slope = 13,746/mo   R2 = 0.62   p = 2.7e-07
next month: 1,336,637  (95% interval 1,124,400 to 1,548,874)
In
# Test: the prediction interval must widen further out
def test_forecast():
    assert (pi[1:] > pi[:-1]).all(), "interval should widen with horizon"
    assert len(yf) == h, "should produce six monthly forecasts"
    assert (yf > 0).all(), "forecast volumes should be positive"
    print("All checks passed")

test_forecast()
Out
All checks passed

Finding: over its contiguous block (December 2011 to June 2014, 30 months) total application volume rises by about 13,700 a month, and the fit is strong and significant (R squared 0.62, p below 0.001). The six-month projection lands near 1.34M with a wide 95% interval of roughly 1.12M to 1.55M.

14.6 Concentration Risk Analysis

Concentration is also a risk question: how exposed is total volume to a handful of accounts? Two lenses are used here, the top-N share and the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI).

In
# Share of total volume held by the largest accounts
n_top1pct = max(1, len(customer_volume) // 100)
shares = {
    'Top 1':  customer_volume.iloc[0]                / total * 100,
    'Top 5':  customer_volume.iloc[:5].sum()         / total * 100,
    'Top 10': customer_volume.iloc[:10].sum()        / total * 100,
    'Top 50': customer_volume.iloc[:50].sum()        / total * 100,
    'Top 1%': customer_volume.iloc[:n_top1pct].sum() / total * 100,
}
for k, v in shares.items():
    print(f"{k:8s}: {v:5.1f}%")

# HHI: sum of squared percentage shares (0 to 10,000)
hhi = ((customer_volume / total * 100) ** 2).sum()
print(f"\nHHI = {hhi:.0f}   (top 1% = {n_top1pct} customers)")
Out
Top 1   :   1.9%
Top 5   :   7.4%
Top 10  :  12.0%
Top 50  :  26.8%
Top 1%  :  40.5%

HHI = 25   (top 1% = 173 customers)
In
# Test: top-N shares are non-decreasing and HHI sits on its scale
def test_concentration():
    vals = [shares['Top 1'], shares['Top 5'], shares['Top 10'], shares['Top 50']]
    assert all(a <= b for a, b in zip(vals, vals[1:])), "wider groups hold at least as much"
    assert 0 <= hhi <= 10_000, "HHI must sit on the 0-10,000 scale"
    print("All checks passed")

test_concentration()
Out
All checks passed

Finding: the largest single customer holds 1.9% of volume and the top ten hold 12%, but the top 1% (173 accounts) hold 40.5%. The HHI is only 25, which on its own scale (where 10,000 is a monopoly) looks negligible. That low reading is misleading here: it is dragged down by the 17,000-strong tail of tiny accounts, so it understates the real exposure. The meaningful measure is the top-decile dependency. Losing or disrupting a small number of large accounts would remove a large slice of volume, so continuity planning should track the top accounts specifically rather than trusting a single market-wide index.

15 · What I'd Investigate Next

  • Customer churn: do the same customers appear across all snapshots, or is there turnover? A pivot of customer presence by date would answer this directly.
  • Search-to-dealing ratio: does the number of OS(W) searches in month N predict DLG volume in month N+1? The data supports this lag analysis.
  • Seasonality: this dataset is too short and too sparse per month to establish seasonal patterns reliably. Would need 5+ full years of uninterrupted monthly data.
  • LA-level growth trends: which local authorities grew fastest? The local_authority file has 36 snapshots (to 2017): enough for a per-LA linear trend across the longer window.
  • Type composition by customer tier: do the top-1% customers over-index on OC1, or do they use all types proportionally? A simple crosstab of customer tier vs type share would answer this.
